mission.title: Microcks: Fix "Failed to Initialize authentication Subsystem" Error
mission.description: Resolves the Keycloak authentication initialization failure that shows 'Failed to Initialize authentication Subsystem' in the Microcks UI. This is one of the most common Microcks deployment issues, typically caused by Keycloak URL misconfiguration, missing client secret, or SSL requirement mismatches.

steps.title: Check Keycloak pod status
steps.description:
Verify the Keycloak pod is running and healthy:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n microcks -l app=keycloak
kubectl logs -n microcks -l app=keycloak --tail=50
```
If the pod is in `CrashLoopBackOff`, check logs for database connection errors. Keycloak requires a working database connection before Microcks can authenticate.

steps.title: Verify Keycloak URL configuration
steps.description:
The Microcks frontend must reach Keycloak at the URL configured in the Helm values. Check the current configuration:
```bash
helm get values microcks -n microcks | grep -A5 keycloak
```
The `keycloak.url` value must be reachable from the browser (not just from within the cluster). Common mistakes:
- Using an internal service URL (e.g., `microcks-keycloak:8080`) instead of an externally accessible URL
- Missing the port number
- Using `http://` when Keycloak requires HTTPS

steps.title: Check Keycloak realm and client configuration
steps.description:
Verify the `microcks` realm exists and the client is configured correctly:
```bash
# Port-forward to Keycloak admin console
kubectl port-forward svc/microcks-keycloak -n microcks 9090:8080 &

# Check realm exists (default: 'microcks')
curl -s http://localhost:9090/realms/microcks | head -5
```
If the realm does not exist, Keycloak initialization failed during first boot. Delete the Keycloak pod to let it re-initialize:
```bash
kubectl delete pod -n microcks -l app=keycloak
```

steps.title: Fix SSL requirement mismatch
steps.description:
A common cause is Keycloak requiring SSL but Microcks connecting over plain HTTP. The Keycloak log shows `SSL is required to authenticate. Remote address X.X.X.X is secure: false`. Fix by setting the SSL requirement to NONE for development:
```bash
# Port-forward to Keycloak
kubectl port-forward svc/microcks-keycloak -n microcks 9090:8080 &

# Get admin token
KC_TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST http://localhost:9090/realms/master/protocol/openid-connect/token \
  -d 'username=admin&password=admin&grant_type=password&client_id=admin-cli' | jq -r '.access_token')

# Set SSL to NONE on the microcks realm
curl -s -X PUT http://localhost:9090/admin/realms/microcks \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $KC_TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"sslRequired": "NONE"}'
```
For production, configure TLS on both Keycloak and Microcks ingresses instead.

steps.title: Verify fix
steps.description:
Restart the Microcks pod to pick up the configuration change:
```bash
kubectl rollout restart deployment/microcks -n microcks
kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod -l app=microcks -n microcks --timeout=120s
```
Then access the Microcks UI and verify the authentication subsystem initializes successfully.

resolution.summary: The 'Failed to Initialize authentication Subsystem' error is resolved by ensuring Keycloak is running, the realm exists, the URL is browser-accessible, and SSL requirements match between Keycloak and Microcks.
codeSnippets: helm get values microcks -n microcks | grep -A5 keycloak
